BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.
8. Functional Requirements
8.1 Customer & KYC
System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.
System shall store multiple customer contact details including phone, email, and address.
System shall maintain KYC profile including status, risk rating, last review and next review date.
System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.
Conceptual draft generated. Review the conceptual ER and use the same /orchestrate endpoint with artifact_id to update relationships or send 'approve' to continue.
Conceptual draft approved and used to generate logical and physical models.

conceptual.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Risk Rating. Canonical table: RISK_RATING. Definition: The internal credit grade assigned as an outcome of a credit assessment. Business purpose: Used to classify borrower or facility risk and support downstream risk processes. Notes: One assessment produces one rating record in this model.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: RISK_RATING represents Risk Rating. Primary key: risk_rating_id. Foreign keys: assessment_id references CREDIT_ASSESSMENT.assessment_id. Business attributes: rating_code means internal grade assigned to the assessed facility; rating_description means text description of the assigned internal grade; rating_date means date on which the rating became effective. Examples: risk_rating_id example R00001; assessment_id example A00001; rating_code example BBB.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Loan Account. Canonical table: LOAN_ACCOUNT. Definition: The operational servicing account associated with a loan. Business purpose: Used to manage balances, repayments, and account-level servicing activity. Notes: Typically one operational account per loan in this model.
Business concept: Default Event. Canonical table: DEFAULT_EVENT. Definition: A formal record that a loan has entered default status. Business purpose: Marks a significant credit deterioration or failure to meet repayment obligations. Notes: A loan may or may not have a default event in this model.
Table summary: CREDIT_ASSESSMENT represents Credit Assessment. Primary key: assessment_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: assessment_date means date on which the assessment was performed; assessment_type means type of assessment such as Initial, Annual Review, or Ad-hoc Review; assessed_by means credit analyst or risk officer responsible for the assessment. Examples: assessment_id example A00001; facility_id example F00001; assessment_date example 2024-01-05.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Delinquency Days (DPD). Canonical table: LOAN_MONITORING. Definition: The number of days a payment obligation is overdue. Business purpose: Used to monitor arrears and identify early warning signs or default risk. Notes: Also referred to as Days Past Due.

conceptual.conceptual_summary: This conceptual model describes the credit lifecycle, starting with a Customer who is granted a credit Facility. This Facility acts as a master agreement under which one or more Loans can be disbursed. Each Loan is serviced via a dedicated Loan Account and is subject to ongoing monitoring for delinquency and potential default events. The risk associated with the parent Facility is managed through periodic Credit Assessments, each yielding a specific Risk Rating, and by calculating Provisions for expected credit losses.
conceptual.diagram_description: The model centers around the Customer, Facility, and Loan entities. A Customer has a one-to-many relationship with Facility. The Facility is a central hub, having one-to-many relationships with Loan, Credit_Assessment, and Provision. The Credit_Assessment has a one-to-one relationship with Risk_Rating. The Loan entity has three distinct relationships: a one-to-one link to a Loan_Account for servicing, a one-to-many link to Loan_Monitoring for tracking arrears, and a one-to-many link to Loan_Default_Event to record defaults.

relationships.description: A Customer is granted one or more credit Facilities.
A Facility can be drawn down into one or more specific Loans.
A Facility undergoes one or more Credit Assessments over its lifetime.
A Facility has periodic Provisions calculated against it.
Each Credit Assessment results in a single Risk Rating.
Each Loan is managed through a single operational Loan Account.
A Loan has its performance tracked through multiple periodic Loan Monitoring records.
A Loan may experience one or more Default Events during its lifecycle.

normalization_notes: Each conceptual entity has been translated into a logical table with a dedicated surrogate primary key (e.g., 'Customer_ID').

Relationships from the conceptual model have been implemented using foreign keys. For example, the 1:N relationship from Customer to Facility is implemented with 'Customer_ID' in the 'Facility' table.

The 1:1 relationships (Loan to Loan_Account, Credit_Assessment to Risk_Rating) are enforced by placing a foreign key in the child table. A unique constraint should be applied to these foreign key columns ('Loan_ID' in Loan_Account, 'Credit_Assessment_ID' in Risk_Rating) to enforce the 1:1 cardinality.

Business-relevant columns not explicitly in the conceptual model were added to provide necessary operational context, such as status fields, business identifiers ('Customer_Identifier', 'Account_Number'), and key dates ('Approval_Date', 'Maturity_Date').
Audit columns ('Created_Date', 'Updated_Date') have been added to each table to track record lifecycle, which is a standard practice in logical data modeling.

ddl:
CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_Name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Customer_Type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Date_Of_Birth DATE NULL,
  Customer_Identifier VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Updated_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_Type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Sanctioned_Amount DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Approval_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Expiry_Date DATE NULL,
  Facility_Status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Updated_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_Amount DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Interest_Rate DECIMAL(10, 4) NOT NULL,
  Disbursement_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Maturity_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Loan_Status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Updated_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Account (
  Loan_Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_Number VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  Account_Open_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Current_Balance DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Account_Status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Account PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Account_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Account_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Credit_Assessment (
  Credit_Assessment_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Assessment_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Assessment_Type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Assessed_By VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Credit_Assessment PRIMARY KEY (Credit_Assessment_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Credit_Assessment_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Risk_Rating (
  Risk_Rating_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Credit_Assessment_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Rating_Code VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  Rating_Description VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  Rating_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Risk_Rating PRIMARY KEY (Risk_Rating_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Risk_Rating_Credit_Assessment_ID FOREIGN KEY (Credit_Assessment_ID) REFERENCES Credit_Assessment (Credit_Assessment_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Reporting_Period_End_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Provision_Amount DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Provision_Type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Monitoring (
  Loan_Monitoring_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Monitoring_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Delinquency_Days INTEGER NOT NULL,
  Outstanding_Principal DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Monitoring PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Monitoring_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Monitoring_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Default_Event (
  Loan_Default_Event_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Default_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Default_Reason VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Cure_Date DATE NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Default_Event PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Default_Event_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Default_Event_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);
CREATE INDEX uidx_customer_identifier ON Customer (Customer_Identifier);
CREATE INDEX idx_facility_customer_id ON Facility (Customer_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_facility_id ON Loan (Facility_ID);

BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.
8. Functional Requirements
8.1 Customer & KYC
System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.
System shall store multiple customer contact details including phone, email, and address.
System shall maintain KYC profile including status, risk rating, last review and next review date.
System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.

model_json.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Risk Rating. Canonical table: RISK_RATING. Definition: The internal credit grade assigned as an outcome of a credit assessment. Business purpose: Used to classify borrower or facility risk and support downstream risk processes. Notes: One assessment produces one rating record in this model.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: RISK_RATING represents Risk Rating. Primary key: risk_rating_id. Foreign keys: assessment_id references CREDIT_ASSESSMENT.assessment_id. Business attributes: rating_code means internal grade assigned to the assessed facility; rating_description means text description of the assigned internal grade; rating_date means date on which the rating became effective. Examples: risk_rating_id example R00001; assessment_id example A00001; rating_code example BBB.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Loan Account. Canonical table: LOAN_ACCOUNT. Definition: The operational servicing account associated with a loan. Business purpose: Used to manage balances, repayments, and account-level servicing activity. Notes: Typically one operational account per loan in this model.
Business concept: Default Event. Canonical table: DEFAULT_EVENT. Definition: A formal record that a loan has entered default status. Business purpose: Marks a significant credit deterioration or failure to meet repayment obligations. Notes: A loan may or may not have a default event in this model.
Table summary: CREDIT_ASSESSMENT represents Credit Assessment. Primary key: assessment_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: assessment_date means date on which the assessment was performed; assessment_type means type of assessment such as Initial, Annual Review, or Ad-hoc Review; assessed_by means credit analyst or risk officer responsible for the assessment. Examples: assessment_id example A00001; facility_id example F00001; assessment_date example 2024-01-05.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Delinquency Days (DPD). Canonical table: LOAN_MONITORING. Definition: The number of days a payment obligation is overdue. Business purpose: Used to monitor arrears and identify early warning signs or default risk. Notes: Also referred to as Days Past Due.

model_json.conceptual_summary: This conceptual model describes the credit lifecycle, starting with a Customer who is granted a credit Facility. This Facility acts as a master agreement under which one or more Loans can be disbursed. Each Loan is serviced via a dedicated Loan Account and is subject to ongoing monitoring for delinquency and potential default events. The risk associated with the parent Facility is managed through periodic Credit Assessments, each yielding a specific Risk Rating, and by calculating Provisions for expected credit losses.
model_json.diagram_description: The model centers around the Customer, Facility, and Loan entities. A Customer has a one-to-many relationship with Facility. The Facility is a central hub, having one-to-many relationships with Loan, Credit_Assessment, and Provision. The Credit_Assessment has a one-to-one relationship with Risk_Rating. The Loan entity has three distinct relationships: a one-to-one link to a Loan_Account for servicing, a one-to-many link to Loan_Monitoring for tracking arrears, and a one-to-many link to Loan_Default_Event to record defaults.

relationships.description: A Customer is granted one or more credit Facilities.
A Facility can be drawn down into one or more specific Loans.
A Facility undergoes one or more Credit Assessments over its lifetime.
A Facility has periodic Provisions calculated against it.
Each Credit Assessment results in a single Risk Rating.
Each Loan is managed through a single operational Loan Account.
A Loan has its performance tracked through multiple periodic Loan Monitoring records.
A Loan may experience one or more Default Events during its lifecycle.

normalization_notes: Each conceptual entity has been translated into a logical table with a dedicated surrogate primary key (e.g., 'Customer_ID').

Relationships from the conceptual model have been implemented using foreign keys. For example, the 1:N relationship from Customer to Facility is implemented with 'Customer_ID' in the 'Facility' table.

The 1:1 relationships (Loan to Loan_Account, Credit_Assessment to Risk_Rating) are enforced by placing a foreign key in the child table. A unique constraint should be applied to these foreign key columns ('Loan_ID' in Loan_Account, 'Credit_Assessment_ID' in Risk_Rating) to enforce the 1:1 cardinality.

Business-relevant columns not explicitly in the conceptual model were added to provide necessary operational context, such as status fields, business identifiers ('Customer_Identifier', 'Account_Number'), and key dates ('Approval_Date', 'Maturity_Date').
Audit columns ('Created_Date', 'Updated_Date') have been added to each table to track record lifecycle, which is a standard practice in logical data modeling.

ddl:
CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_Name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Customer_Type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Date_Of_Birth DATE NULL,
  Customer_Identifier VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Updated_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_Type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Sanctioned_Amount DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Approval_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Expiry_Date DATE NULL,
  Facility_Status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Updated_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_Amount DECIMAL(18, 2) NOT NULL,
  Interest_Rate DECIMAL(10, 4) NOT NULL,
  Disbursement_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Maturity_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Loan_Status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Created_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Updated_Date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Account (

BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.
8. Functional Requirements
8.1 Customer & KYC
System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.
System shall store multiple customer contact details including phone, email, and address.
System shall maintain KYC profile including status, risk rating, last review and next review date.
System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.

conceptual.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Risk Rating. Canonical table: RISK_RATING. Definition: The internal credit grade assigned as an outcome of a credit assessment. Business purpose: Used to classify borrower or facility risk and support downstream risk processes. Notes: One assessment produces one rating record in this model.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: RISK_RATING represents Risk Rating. Primary key: risk_rating_id. Foreign keys: assessment_id references CREDIT_ASSESSMENT.assessment_id. Business attributes: rating_code means internal grade assigned to the assessed facility; rating_description means text description of the assigned internal grade; rating_date means date on which the rating became effective. Examples: risk_rating_id example R00001; assessment_id example A00001; rating_code example BBB.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Loan Account. Canonical table: LOAN_ACCOUNT. Definition: The operational servicing account associated with a loan. Business purpose: Used to manage balances, repayments, and account-level servicing activity. Notes: Typically one operational account per loan in this model.
Business concept: Default Event. Canonical table: DEFAULT_EVENT. Definition: A formal record that a loan has entered default status. Business purpose: Marks a significant credit deterioration or failure to meet repayment obligations. Notes: A loan may or may not have a default event in this model.
Table summary: CREDIT_ASSESSMENT represents Credit Assessment. Primary key: assessment_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: assessment_date means date on which the assessment was performed; assessment_type means type of assessment such as Initial, Annual Review, or Ad-hoc Review; assessed_by means credit analyst or risk officer responsible for the assessment. Examples: assessment_id example A00001; facility_id example F00001; assessment_date example 2024-01-05.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Delinquency Days (DPD). Canonical table: LOAN_MONITORING. Definition: The number of days a payment obligation is overdue. Business purpose: Used to monitor arrears and identify early warning signs or default risk. Notes: Also referred to as Days Past Due.

conceptual.conceptual_summary: This conceptual data model is derived strictly from the provided glossary, which focuses on the credit and lending domain. It establishes the core entities and their relationships for managing customer credit exposure, from sanctioned facilities to individual loans and their operational accounts. The model also incorporates credit risk management aspects such as credit assessments, risk ratings, provisions for expected losses, tracking of loan default events, and monitoring for delinquency. It's important to note that this model does not cover general core banking concepts like deposit accounts, generic transactions, fees, or general ledger postings, as these were not present in the provided glossary.
conceptual.diagram_description: The diagram illustrates the relationships within the credit and lending domain. A 'Customer' can have multiple 'Facilities'. Each 'Facility' can generate multiple 'Loans'. Each 'Loan' is associated with a single 'Loan Account' and may have a 'Loan Default Event'. 'Facilities' undergo 'Credit Assessments', which in turn produce a 'Risk Rating'. 'Facilities' can also have multiple 'Provisions' for expected losses. 'Loans' are subject to 'Loan Monitoring', which tracks delinquency over time.

relationships.description: A Customer can have one or more Facilities.
A Facility can generate one or more Loans.
A Loan typically has one associated Loan Account.
A Loan may or may not have a Loan Default Event.
A Facility undergoes one or more Credit Assessments.
A Credit Assessment produces one Risk Rating.
A Facility can have multiple Provisions over time.
A Loan is monitored for delinquency, resulting in multiple Loan Monitoring records over time.
A customer has a CIBIL report.

normalization_notes: All tables are designed with surrogate primary keys for consistency and flexibility.
Foreign keys are implemented to represent all conceptual relationships, ensuring referential integrity.
No many-to-many relationships were identified in the provided conceptual model, thus no associative tables were required.
Common business-relevant columns such as 'created_date', 'updated_date', and 'is_active' have been added to tables for auditing and status tracking.

For 1:1 relationships (Loan:Loan Account, Credit Assessment:Risk Rating, Customer:CIBIL), separate tables are maintained with foreign keys to preserve distinct entity concerns and allow for future changes in cardinality.

For the 1:0..1 relationship between Loan and Loan Default Event, the 'Loan_ID' serves as both the primary key and foreign key in the 'Loan_Default_Event' table, enforcing that a loan can have at most one default event record.

CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  customer_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  date_of_birth DATE NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  facility_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  facility_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  sanction_date DATE NOT NULL,
  sanction_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  currency_code CHAR(3) NOT NULL,
  facility_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  loan_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  interest_rate DECIMAL(9, 4) NOT NULL,
  disbursement_date DATE NOT NULL,
  loan_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  loan_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  tenure_months INTEGER NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Account (
  Loan_Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  account_number VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  account_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  opening_date DATE NOT NULL,
  closing_date DATE NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Account PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Account_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Account_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Credit_Assessment (
  Credit_Assessment_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  assessment_date DATE NOT NULL,
  assessment_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  assessment_outcome VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  assessor_name VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Credit_Assessment PRIMARY KEY (Credit_Assessment_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Credit_Assessment_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Risk_Rating (
  Risk_Rating_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Credit_Assessment_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  rating_code VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  rating_description VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  rating_date DATE NOT NULL,
  rating_model_version VARCHAR(50) NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Risk_Rating PRIMARY KEY (Risk_Rating_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Risk_Rating_Credit_Assessment_ID FOREIGN KEY (Credit_Assessment_ID) REFERENCES Credit_Assessment (Credit_Assessment_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  provision_date DATE NOT NULL,
  provision_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  provision_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  accounting_period VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Default_Event (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  default_date DATE NOT NULL,
  default_reason VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  default_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Default_Event PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Default_Event_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Monitoring (
  Loan_Monitoring_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  monitoring_date DATE NOT NULL,
  delinquency_days INTEGER NOT NULL,
  arrears_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NULL,
  early_warning_flag BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  monitoring_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Monitoring PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Monitoring_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Monitoring_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE CIBIL_Report (
  CIBIL_Report_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  report_date DATE NOT NULL,
  cibil_score INTEGER NULL,
  report_reference_number VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_CIBIL_Report PRIMARY KEY (CIBIL_Report_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_CIBIL_Report_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_loan_facility_id ON Loan (Facility_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_account_loan_id ON Loan_Account (Loan_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_credit_assessment_facility_id ON Credit_Assessment (Facility_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_risk_rating_credit_assessment_id ON Risk_Rating (Credit_Assessment_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_provision_facility_id ON Provision (Facility_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_default_event_loan_id ON Loan_Default_Event (Loan_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_monitoring_loan_id ON Loan_Monitoring (Loan_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_cibil_report_customer_id ON CIBIL_Report (Customer_ID);

BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.
8. Functional Requirements
8.1 Customer & KYC
System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.
System shall store multiple customer contact details including phone, email, and address.
System shall maintain KYC profile including status, risk rating, last review and next review date.
System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.

model_json.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Risk Rating. Canonical table: RISK_RATING. Definition: The internal credit grade assigned as an outcome of a credit assessment. Business purpose: Used to classify borrower or facility risk and support downstream risk processes. Notes: One assessment produces one rating record in this model.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: RISK_RATING represents Risk Rating. Primary key: risk_rating_id. Foreign keys: assessment_id references CREDIT_ASSESSMENT.assessment_id. Business attributes: rating_code means internal grade assigned to the assessed facility; rating_description means text description of the assigned internal grade; rating_date means date on which the rating became effective. Examples: risk_rating_id example R00001; assessment_id example A00001; rating_code example BBB.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Loan Account. Canonical table: LOAN_ACCOUNT. Definition: The operational servicing account associated with a loan. Business purpose: Used to manage balances, repayments, and account-level servicing activity. Notes: Typically one operational account per loan in this model.
Business concept: Default Event. Canonical table: DEFAULT_EVENT. Definition: A formal record that a loan has entered default status. Business purpose: Marks a significant credit deterioration or failure to meet repayment obligations. Notes: A loan may or may not have a default event in this model.
Table summary: CREDIT_ASSESSMENT represents Credit Assessment. Primary key: assessment_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: assessment_date means date on which the assessment was performed; assessment_type means type of assessment such as Initial, Annual Review, or Ad-hoc Review; assessed_by means credit analyst or risk officer responsible for the assessment. Examples: assessment_id example A00001; facility_id example F00001; assessment_date example 2024-01-05.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Delinquency Days (DPD). Canonical table: LOAN_MONITORING. Definition: The number of days a payment obligation is overdue. Business purpose: Used to monitor arrears and identify early warning signs or default risk. Notes: Also referred to as Days Past Due.

model_json.conceptual_summary: This conceptual data model is derived strictly from the provided glossary, which focuses on the credit and lending domain. It establishes the core entities and their relationships for managing customer credit exposure, from sanctioned facilities to individual loans and their operational accounts. The model also incorporates credit risk management aspects such as credit assessments, risk ratings, provisions for expected losses, tracking of loan default events, and monitoring for delinquency. It's important to note that this model does not cover general core banking concepts like deposit accounts, generic transactions, fees, or general ledger postings, as these were not present in the provided glossary.
model_json.diagram_description: The diagram illustrates the relationships within the credit and lending domain. A 'Customer' can have multiple 'Facilities'. Each 'Facility' can generate multiple 'Loans'. Each 'Loan' is associated with a single 'Loan Account' and may have a 'Loan Default Event'. 'Facilities' undergo 'Credit Assessments', which in turn produce a 'Risk Rating'. 'Facilities' can also have multiple 'Provisions' for expected losses. 'Loans' are subject to 'Loan Monitoring', which tracks delinquency over time.

normalization_notes: All tables are designed with surrogate primary keys for consistency and flexibility.
Foreign keys are implemented to represent all conceptual relationships, ensuring referential integrity.
No many-to-many relationships were identified in the provided conceptual model, thus no associative tables were required.
Common business-relevant columns such as 'created_date', 'updated_date', and 'is_active' have been added to tables for auditing and status tracking.

For 1:1 relationships (Loan:Loan Account, Credit Assessment:Risk Rating, Customer:CIBIL), separate tables are maintained with foreign keys to preserve distinct entity concerns and allow for future changes in cardinality.

For the 1:0..1 relationship between Loan and Loan Default Event, the 'Loan_ID' serves as both the primary key and foreign key in the 'Loan_Default_Event' table, enforcing that a loan can have at most one default event record.

ddl:
CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  customer_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  date_of_birth DATE NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  facility_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  facility_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  sanction_date DATE NOT NULL,
  sanction_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  currency_code CHAR(3) NOT NULL,
  facility_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  loan_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  interest_rate DECIMAL(9, 4) NOT NULL,
  disbursement_date DATE NOT NULL,
  loan_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  loan_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  tenure_months INTEGER NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  is_active BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Account (
  Loan_Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  account_number VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  account_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  opening_date DATE NOT NULL,
  closing_date DATE NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Account PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Account_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Account_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Credit_Assessment (
  Credit_Assessment_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  assessment_date DATE NOT NULL,
  assessment_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  assessment_outcome VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  assessor_name VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Credit_Assessment PRIMARY KEY (Credit_Assessment_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Credit_Assessment_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Risk_Rating (
  Risk_Rating_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Credit_Assessment_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  rating_code VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  rating_description VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  rating_date DATE NOT NULL,
  rating_model_version VARCHAR(50) NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Risk_Rating PRIMARY KEY (Risk_Rating_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Risk_Rating_Credit_Assessment_ID FOREIGN KEY (Credit_Assessment_ID) REFERENCES Credit_Assessment (Credit_Assessment_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  provision_date DATE NOT NULL,
  provision_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  provision_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  accounting_period VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Default_Event (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  default_date DATE NOT NULL,
  default_reason VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  default_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Default_Event PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Default_Event_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan_Monitoring (
  Loan_Monitoring_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  monitoring_date DATE NOT NULL,
  delinquency_days INTEGER NOT NULL,
  arrears_amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NULL,
  early_warning_flag BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  monitoring_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan_Monitoring PRIMARY KEY (Loan_Monitoring_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Monitoring_Loan_ID FOREIGN KEY (Loan_ID) REFERENCES Loan (Loan_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE CIBIL_Report (
  CIBIL_Report_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  report_date DATE NOT NULL,
  cibil_score INTEGER NULL,
  report_reference_number VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  created_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  updated_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_CIBIL_Report PRIMARY KEY (CIBIL_Report_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_CIBIL_Report_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);
CREATE INDEX idx_facility_customer_id ON Facility (Customer_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_facility_id ON Loan (Facility_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_account_loan_id ON Loan_Account (Loan_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_credit_assessment_facility_id ON Credit_Assessment (Facility_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_risk_rating_credit_assessment_id ON Risk_Rating (Credit_Assessment_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_provision_facility_id ON Provision (Facility_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_default_event_loan_id ON Loan_Default_Event (Loan_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_loan_monitoring_loan_id ON Loan_Monitoring (Loan_ID);
CREATE INDEX idx_cibil_report_customer_id ON CIBIL_Report (Customer_ID);
